metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hardcoded count `5` in all ACs (and test assertions) with a reference to a single source-of-truth constant (e.g., `len(ALL_ENSURE_UNITS)` or a registry-exported list), or explicitly document that the count must be updated whenever a unit is added/removed.
findings.finding: The plan hardcodes the unit count '5' across multiple ACs ('reports 5 changed', 'ensures: 0/5 applied', '5/5 after --repair', 'records all five ids', 'five ids') but the Why section explicitly anticipates 'as units are added or changed'. When a sixth unit lands, every one of these ACs silently becomes wrong, and the tests will either fail spuriously or miss the new unit — undermining the stated goal of re-validating 'as units are added or changed'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('records all five ids'), AC4 ('reports 5 changed'), AC5 ('ensures: 0/5 applied', '5/5 after --repair')
findings.suggested_fix: Export a module-level constant `ENSURE_UNIT_IDS: tuple[str, ...] = (...)` from the ensure-registry and derive the expected count in the tests as `len(ENSURE_UNIT_IDS)`. Update AC3/AC4/AC5 to read 'records all N ids' / 'reports N changed' where N = len(ENSURE_UNIT_IDS).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'integration test' framing in the title/Why with AC1's 'default tier' requirement — either rename the suite (e.g. 'hermetic drift-matrix tests') or add explicit guidance that no @pytest.mark.integration marker should be applied.
findings.finding: The document title and 'Why' section frame the suite as 'hermetic drift-matrix integration tests', implying it belongs in an integration test tier (e.g. marked @pytest.mark.integration), while AC1 explicitly requires it to 'run in the default (non-integration/non-external) tier'. An implementer reading the title/Why will apply integration markers; one reading AC1 will omit them — the two sections contradict each other.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the document title to 'Hermetic drift-matrix tests for the ensure-registry' and add a one-line note in the What or AC1 clarifying: 'No pytest marker needed; the suite is hermetic and runs unconditionally in the default tier.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the tier-naming contradiction: either rename the suite (e.g., 'hermetic drift-matrix store tests') or explicitly state which pytest marker (if any) applies so implementers unambiguously know the correct tier.
findings.finding: The plan title calls these 'integration tests' but AC item 1 specifies they must run in the 'default (non-integration/non-external) tier'. These two statements contradict each other: an implementer following the title may apply `@pytest.mark.integration` markers (causing the tests to be skipped in the default tier), while an implementer following the AC will not mark them, making the 'integration test' label misleading and the tier placement ambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'integration tests' in the title with 'hermetic store tests' (or 'unit-level parametrized tests'), and add a note in AC item 1 such as 'no `@pytest.mark.integration` marker — these run unconditionally in the default `pytest` invocation'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the operator `fsck --repair` AC and pre-feature-store AC belong in `test_ensure_drift_matrix.py` or should be cross-referenced to the already-covering tests in `test_fsck_ensures.py` to avoid YAGNI duplication (cite `test_repair_converges_pending_store`, `test_repair_ensure_phase_is_idempotent`, and `test_plain_fsck_pending_line_and_no_sweep`).
findings.finding: AC lines for the operator `fsck --repair` path (reports 5 changed; second --repair reports 0 changed / no new commits) and the pre-feature store scenario (0/5 → 5/5 via read-only fsck then --repair) duplicate tests already present in test_fsck_ensures.py, making those two ACs YAGNI additions to this new file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Operator path: rebar fsck --repair...' and 'Pre-feature store (no .ensure-applied)...'
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the operator fsck and pre-feature ACs as 'tested by cross-reference to test_fsck_ensures.py' and remove them from the new file's AC list, keeping the new file focused on the novel multi-unit combination matrix (S1–S8) that has no existing coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit scenarios S0, S1, S3, S4 and ACs 4–5 against the already-landed analogs (test_gc_config.py, test_gitattributes_merge.py, test_ensure_invariants.py, test_fsck_ensures.py) and either (a) remove/trim the redundant single-unit sub-scenarios from the new matrix, replacing them with a comment citing the prior-art test, or (b) explicitly justify why duplicating them in a combined fixture adds value (e.g. cross-checking that a multi-unit fixture doesn't interfere with single-unit assertions).
findings.finding: Several named scenarios and ACs in the plan are already covered by existing landed tests, but the plan proposes adding them again without acknowledging or deferring to those analogs — creating planned duplication rather than additive coverage.
findings.location: Scenarios list (S0, S1, S3, S4) and AC4/AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the parametrized matrix to the genuinely novel combinations only (S6: gc+gitignore, S7: three-behind, S8: fully-legacy five-unit, plus a minimal S0 converged baseline as a sanity anchor). For S1/S3/S4, add a comment like `# single-unit coverage owned by test_gc_config.py / test_gitattributes_merge.py / test_ensure_invariants.py`. Drop AC4/AC5 from this plan and cite test_fsck_ensures.py instead, unless the intent is to additionally exercise --repair against a multi-unit-drifted (not just marker-absent) store — in which case state that delta explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'over-cap module' (e.g. 'the file must stay under N lines per the project lint config') and 'drift-gate regressions' (e.g. 'run `pytest tests/interfaces/store/ -m drift_gate` must stay green') in AC6 so the criterion is self-contained and measurable.
findings.finding: AC6 references 'no over-cap module' and 'no drift-gate regressions' — two project-internal terms that are undefined in the plan. An executor cannot evaluate these constraints without knowing what the module line-count cap is or which regressions constitute the 'drift gate', making AC6 partially non-measurable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: '`make lint` + `make typecheck` clean; the new file adds no over-cap module and no drift-gate regressions'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the last AC bullet with: '`make lint` + `make typecheck` pass; the new file stays under the project module line cap (currently N lines, enforced by `make lint`); `pytest tests/interfaces/store/ -m drift_gate` (or the equivalent existing suite name) reports zero regressions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2's gitattributes post-repair invariant to specify the expected replacement content (e.g. '`.gitattributes` contains the current required `merge=ours` pattern after repair') or explicitly state that no replacement is written and removal is the complete invariant.
findings.finding: AC2 states the post-repair gitattributes invariant as 'no retired .gitattributes line' (removal only). For a migration criterion the plan should verify both removal of the old content AND presence of whatever replacement content the unit is expected to write — matching the pattern used for gitignore ('gitignore ⊇ the expected set'). If the gitattributes unit rewrites additional lines, the test will not catch regressions in that replacement content.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` to the per-scenario fixture teardown (or call it immediately after each drift-fabrication step and after each `run_ensures` call) so the per-process pending cache does not leak between parametrized scenarios.
findings.finding: The plan never mentions ensures._reset_pending_cache(), but every existing test that fabricates drift by manipulating .ensure-applied calls this hook before re-running run_ensures in the same process. Without it, the parametrized matrix scenarios share a process-level _pending_cache keyed by tracker path, causing earlier scenarios to poison later ones with stale 'all-applied' or 'all-pending' cache entries.
findings.location: What section / Scenarios paragraph; AC2 (idempotency assertion); AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the shared fixture or at the start of each parametrized test body, call `from rebar._store import ensures; ensures._reset_pending_cache()` after fabricating drift and again after each `run_ensures` invocation, mirroring the pattern in test_fsck_ensures.py and test_ensure_invariants.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` to the per-scenario fixture teardown (or after each drift-fabrication step) so the process-level pending-hint cache cannot bleed across parametrized scenarios.
findings.finding: The plan does not mention calling `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` after fabricating drift in each parametrized scenario fixture. Existing analogous tests (test_ensure_invariants.py:36, test_fsck_ensures.py:85) consistently call this hook after mutating tracker state. Under parametrized execution, the process-level pending-hint cache populated by an earlier scenario (e.g. S8 fully-legacy with 5 pending units) can bleed into later scenarios (e.g. S0 converged), causing the hint-related branch of maybe_emit_pending_hint to fire incorrectly — a hidden intra-test ordering assumption that produces flaky or wrong results depending on pytest scenario ordering.
findings.location: What section / drift-fabrication fixture setup (all scenarios S0–S8)
findings.suggested_fix: In the shared fixture that fabricates drift, add `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` immediately after mutating the tracker state (after the git commands that inject drift and after each `run_ensures` call that reconverges it), matching the pattern in `test_ensure_invariants.py:36` and `test_fsck_ensures.py:85`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the 'What' section explaining why real-tracker post-hoc git mutation (vs. fixture bundles or mock-registry init) is the chosen approach — e.g. it produces authentic drift shapes, stays aligned with existing test patterns, and avoids fixture staleness.
findings.finding: The plan states the positive rationale for the test gap ('no single suite exercises the differential contract') but provides no positive rationale for WHY the chosen fabrication approach — init a real tracker then post-hoc mutate git state — is the right mechanism, as opposed to alternatives (e.g. pre-built fixture bundles, or a mock-registry init path). The plan's 'Why' section addresses the gap, not the approach fitness.
findings.location: Why section / What section — no 'approach rationale' paragraph

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
